standardization_map.colloquial: how to work in this action

concepts.name: Topological Sort
concepts.description: A linear ordering of graph vertices such that for every directed edge from vertex u to v, u comes before v in the ordering.

concepts.name: Graph Data Structure
concepts.description: A non-linear data structure consisting of nodes (vertices) and edges, representing relationships between them.

concepts.name: Vertex
concepts.description: A fundamental unit or node in a graph.

concepts.name: Directed Edge
concepts.description: An edge in a graph that has a specific direction, indicating a one-way relationship from a source vertex to a target vertex.

concepts.name: Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)
concepts.description: A directed graph that contains no cycles, meaning there is no way to start at a vertex and follow a sequence of directed edges back to that same vertex.

concepts.name: Cycle (in a graph)
concepts.description: A path in a graph that starts and ends at the same vertex, where no edge is repeated.

concepts.name: Time Complexity
concepts.description: A measure of the amount of time taken by an algorithm to run as a function of the length of its input.

concepts.name: Space Complexity
concepts.description: A measure of the amount of memory an algorithm requires to run as a function of the length of its input.

concepts.name: Uniqueness of Topological Sort
concepts.description: A property stating that a topological sort is not necessarily unique for a given DAG; multiple valid orderings can exist.

concepts.name: Depth-First Search (DFS)
concepts.description: A graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.

concepts.name: Stack Data Structure
concepts.description: A linear data structure that follows the Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) principle, where the last element added is the first one to be removed.

concepts.name: Graph Traversal State: Visited/Unvisited
concepts.description: The state of a node in a graph during traversal, indicating whether it has already been processed (visited) or not (unvisited).

concepts.name: Khan's Algorithm
concepts.description: An algorithm for topological sorting that uses a queue and counts in-degrees of nodes.

concepts.name: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
concepts.description: A graph traversal algorithm that explores all the neighbor nodes at the present depth level before moving on to nodes at the next depth level.

concepts.name: Graph Algorithms
concepts.description: A set of algorithms designed to solve problems on graphs, such as finding paths, traversals, or orderings.

prerequisites.rationale: Understanding what a graph is, its components, and its representation is fundamental to grasping any graph algorithm like Topological Sort.
Vertices are the fundamental units that are being ordered in a topological sort.
Topological Sort's definition explicitly relies on the presence and direction of edges to define ordering constraints.
Topological Sort is uniquely applicable to Directed Acyclic Graphs; understanding DAGs is a strict prerequisite.
Understanding what a cycle is in a graph is necessary to comprehend the 'acyclic' property of a DAG.
DFS is the primary algorithm demonstrated in the lecture for computing a topological sort.
A stack is a key data structure used in the DFS-based approach for topological sorting to store nodes in the correct order.
The DFS algorithm relies on marking nodes as visited or unvisited to avoid infinite loops and process each node once.
Understanding time complexity is important for evaluating the efficiency of the Topological Sort algorithm.
Understanding space complexity is important for evaluating the memory usage of the Topological Sort algorithm.
